summary: Dan Shapiro compiles his best resources on running a successful Kickstarter campaign, drawn from his experience with Robot Turtles — the most-backed board game in Kickstarter history, raising over $631K. The post serves as a curated guide linking to his detailed writings and interviews covering campaign construction, video strategy, shipping logistics, production, and timing. He frames the Robot Turtles campaign as an unexpected blockbuster that started as a fun diversion. The collection spans mid-campaign data analysis, post-campaign video breakdowns, logistics deep-dives, and podcast interviews, offering a comprehensive playbook for aspiring Kickstarter creators.
key_points: Robot Turtles became the most-backed board game in Kickstarter history despite Shapiro viewing it initially as a fun diversion, not a serious venture
Successful Kickstarter campaigns require deliberate strategy across multiple dimensions: video production, campaign structure, timing, and shipping logistics
Shipping logistics is highlighted as one of the hardest parts of Kickstarter — Robot Turtles required 3 semi-trucks, shipping to 65 countries, totaling 36 tons
Transparency about real campaign data (traffic sources, amounts) is presented as valuable to the Kickstarter community
money_quotes: I worked hard on the Robot Turtles campaign, but fundamentally thought of it as a fun diversion. I had no idea it was going to turn into the blockbuster that it did!
Shipping logistics is one of the hardest parts of Kickstarter campaigns.
3 semis, 65 countries, 36 tons: Shipping my Kickstarter.
key_insight: A successful Kickstarter campaign requires deliberate strategy across video, structure, timing, and logistics — and sharing those lessons transparently helps the entire creator community.
